title: Lemon drizzle traybake
subtitle: A sharp lemon syrup-soaked sponge cut into squares
excerpt: Sixteen squares of all-in-one sponge soaked while still hot with a sharp lemon syrup that soaks through and sets to a thin crust. Keeps well in a tin for four days and cuts cleanly at room temperature.
sourceNotes: Lemon sponge cake and lemon syrup applications documented in pre-1928 British household cookery; the drizzle-and-soak technique appears in early-twentieth-century British baking columns in the British Newspaper Archive. The all-in-one sponge method, combining all ingredients in one bowl, is described in pre-1928 domestic science guides as a simplified creaming alternative. Cross-referenced with Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze individual squares. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps well wrapped for 4 days. The lemon crust improves the next day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 185°C. The sponge is done when a skewer comes out clean and the top springs back when pressed.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin lemon and sugar syrup poured over a hot sponge immediately after it comes out of the oven. The heat drives the syrup into the crumb, keeping it moist and setting a faintly crystalline crust on the surface. The ratio of lemon to sugar determines the tartness: more sugar gives a sweeter crust; more lemon juice gives a sharper, more translucent glaze. The cake must be hot for the syrup to penetrate; pouring it on a cold sponge leaves a sticky surface rather than soaking through.

content.text: Lemon drizzle traybake is a straightforward all-in-one sponge finished with a
poured over the hot cake the moment it comes out of the oven. The syrup sinks in and sets to a thin, faintly crunchy crust. Sixteen squares, mixed and in the oven in under 20 minutes.

Heat the oven to 175°C fan (185°C conventional). Grease and line the tin with baking paper. Put the flour, 200 g of sugar, butter, eggs, baking powder, milk, and lemon zest into a large bowl and beat together until smooth and just combined, about 2 minutes by hand or 1 minute with an electric mixer. Do not overbeat. Pour into the prepared tin and level the top.
Bake for 28 to 32 minutes, until the surface is pale golden, a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean, and the sponge springs back when pressed lightly.

While the cake is in the oven, stir the lemon juice and 80 g of sugar together in a small bowl or jug; the sugar will not fully dissolve. Remove the hot cake from the oven and immediately poke it all over with a skewer. Pour the lemon syrup evenly over the surface; it will soak in and the sugar will form a faint crust as it cools. Leave the cake in the tin for 10 minutes, then transfer to a wire rack. Cut into squares once fully cold.
